strengths: All validation criteria are fully implemented in the correct order: TypeError for None/non-string input, JSONDecodeError caught and re-raised as ValueError, root dict check, Resources key presence check, Resources dict type check, and non-empty Resources check
Docstring is comprehensive, documenting the input format, return value, and every exception that can be raised with descriptive messages
Type hints are correctly applied (template_str: str) -> list[str]) and the return value correctly uses list(resources.keys()) to return logical IDs as a list of strings
weaknesses: The TypeError check is slightly redundant — `template_str is None` is already covered by `not isinstance(template_str, str)` since None is not a str, though this is a minor style point and does not affect correctness
No edge case handling for non-string keys in the Resources dict (though CloudFormation logical IDs are always strings, so this is a negligible concern in practice)
The error message for TypeError could be slightly more descriptive by including the expected type explicitly, though the current message is already clear and meets the criteria
task: Parse an AWS CloudFormation template in JSON format and extract all resource logical IDs

strengths: BUCKET_PATTERN compiled at module level as a named constant and reused in the function body without any re.compile() or raw string literal inside the function — satisfies the module-level compilation requirement

Single regex combines length bounds (3-63 via [a-z0-9]{1,61} middle + 2 anchor chars = 3-63 total), allowed character set, start/end constraints, and consecutive-hyphen prohibition via (?!.*--) negative lookahead in one pattern
Docstring clearly lists all validation rules including the note that IPv4 is inherently satisfied by dot exclusion, and a dedicated IP_PATTERN constant is included as a safeguard
weaknesses: The regex length calculation is slightly off: [a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]{1,61}[a-z0-9] matches 3 to 63 characters correctly (1+1-61+1=3-63), but a single-character middle section means minimum is 3 and maximum is 63 — this is actually correct, but worth verifying edge cases like exactly 3-char names (e.g., 'ab' fails, 'abc' passes — correct)
No explicit handling of non-string input (e.g., None or integer) which could raise an AttributeError on .match(); a type guard or isinstance check would make the function more robust

The IP_PATTERN safeguard check is technically redundant since dots are already excluded by BUCKET_PATTERN, and since no bucket name passing BUCKET_PATTERN could ever match IP_PATTERN, the second check can never return False — it works correctly but adds dead code
task: Write a Python function to validate AWS S3 bucket names according to AWS naming rules

strengths: Correctly implements all three AWS partition named capture groups and validates account_id as exactly 12 digits
The match_arn function properly handles None, empty string, and non-string inputs by returning None without raising exceptions
All required unit tests are present including the consecutive slashes test, and tests are properly guarded with if __name__ == '__main__' so they don't execute at import time
weaknesses: The role_name character class uses [a-zA-Z0-9_-] which omits the forward slash from the base segment class — while the pattern correctly prevents consecutive slashes via the segment structure, the criteria explicitly requires the character class to be written as [a-zA-Z0-9_/\-] (with forward slash included in the class), not split across the alternation pattern

The regex does not include forward slash in the character class as specified: criteria states 'the character class must be explicitly written as [a-zA-Z0-9_/\-]' but the solution uses [a-zA-Z0-9_-]+ with slash only as a separator
Minor: the solution returns a Match object (not None) on success, which is acceptable per the criteria, but the function could be more explicit about its return type contract
task: Create a regex pattern to match valid AWS IAM role ARNs

strengths: All type hints are correctly implemented including Union[dict, str] and _depth: int = 0 in the signature, fully satisfying that criterion
TypeError is raised unconditionally at every recursive call entry (before the depth check and before parsing), ensuring non-dict values produce clean TypeErrors rather than AttributeErrors
Docstring is comprehensive, covering all parameters including _depth with type/default/purpose, return type, all exceptions, and an EC2 metadata example with correct output
weaknesses: After parsing a JSON string, the code raises TypeError if the parsed result is not a dict, but the error message says 'got {type}' which could be misleading since the original input was a valid JSON string — a more descriptive message distinguishing this case would improve clarity

The recursion depth guard checks _depth >= 20 before parsing the string, meaning a string input at depth 20 raises RecursionError rather than potentially a ValueError for invalid JSON — this ordering is correct per spec but the interaction is subtle and undocumented

No handling for list values in the metadata — if a JSON array appears as a value it is stored as-is rather than being recursively flattened with numeric indices, which may be acceptable but is not explicitly documented as a design decision
task: Write a Python function to convert AWS EC2 instance metadata JSON response into a flattened dictionary with dot-notation keys

strengths: Correctly uses a single outer capture group wrapping the full 20-character key, satisfying group(1) requirement
Properly implements negative lookbehind (?<![A-Z0-9]) and negative lookahead (?![A-Z0-9]) to prevent matching keys embedded in longer alphanumeric strings
Uses non-capturing alternation (?:AKIA|ASIA|AROA|AIDA) for all four required prefixes followed by exactly [A-Z0-9]{16}, and demonstrates all required test cases including boundary rejection
weaknesses: The solution does not explicitly verify or document that re.IGNORECASE is not used, though it is indeed absent from the code
Test cases could include lowercase variants (e.g., 'akiaiosfodnn7example') to explicitly demonstrate case-sensitivity rejection
Minor: no explicit assertion/validation logic to programmatically confirm pass/fail, relying only on print output
task: Create a regex pattern to extract AWS access key IDs from logs or configuration files

strengths: Fully satisfies all specified criteria including None check before general type check, explicit boolean exclusion, named MAX_TIMESTAMP_MS constant inside function body, and correct UTC datetime construction
Complete NumPy-style docstring documents parameter, return value, and both exception types with accurate descriptions
Correct ISO 8601 output format using strftime with '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ' producing consistent strings without microseconds or UTC offset suffixes
weaknesses: No meaningful weaknesses; the solution precisely matches every requirement in the criteria
Minor style note: the TypeError message 'timestamp_ms must be an int' could be slightly more descriptive (e.g., mentioning booleans are excluded), but this is not required by the criteria
Edge case of timestamp_ms == 0 (Unix epoch) is implicitly valid but not explicitly tested or mentioned in docstring, though this is not a deficiency per the stated criteria
task: Write a Python function to parse AWS CloudWatch log timestamps and convert them to a human-readable format

strengths: All required fields (FunctionName, Runtime, Handler, Role) are correctly defined with proper patterns, maxLength constraints, and data types matching the specification exactly

VpcConfig, EphemeralStorage, Environment, Timeout, and MemorySize are all implemented correctly with proper nested structures, minItems, min/max constraints, patternProperties, additionalProperties:false, and maxProperties as specified
Top-level additionalProperties is set to false, and the Runtime enum includes a comprehensive and accurate set of valid AWS Lambda runtime strings
weaknesses: VpcConfig is missing additionalProperties:false, which could allow unknown fields within the VpcConfig object to pass validation silently
EphemeralStorage is also missing additionalProperties:false on its object definition, leaving it open to extra properties

The Environment object itself does not require 'Variables' as a required property, and Variables is not marked as required within Environment, though this may be intentional for optional usage - however the Environment object also lacks additionalProperties:false to prevent extra sibling keys alongside Variables
task: Create a JSON schema that validates the structure of an AWS Lambda function configuration object

strengths: Comprehensive docstring covering all required elements: supported types, unsupported types, single-key evaluation behavior, exceptions, and parameter/return descriptions
Correct use of decimal.Decimal for N type comparisons with both stored and condition values converted consistently, avoiding float precision issues
Clean implementation that explicitly extracts the first key via next(iter(typed_value)) rather than implicit loop behavior, satisfying the intentional design requirement
weaknesses: The TypeError note in the docstring is slightly misleading — it says 'silently skipped' but the implementation returns False from _matches and filters via isinstance check, which is correct behavior but the docstring implies a TypeError might be raised when it never actually is
The redundant isinstance(item, dict) check in the list comprehension (already checked inside _matches) is minor code duplication, though harmless

No explicit handling documented for when condition_value cannot be converted to Decimal for N-type comparisons (e.g., passing a non-numeric string), which could raise an unhandled decimal.InvalidOperation exception
task: Write a Python function to filter AWS DynamoDB table items based on a simple attribute condition

strengths: Correctly uses two distinct alternation branches for GovCloud (three-segment) and standard (two-segment) formats, with GovCloud listed first to prevent partial matches
Uses named capture group (?P<region>...) that isolates only the region code, excluding surrounding dots or URL components
Right boundary correctly requires \.amazonaws\.com immediately after the region, preventing false positives on non-endpoint URL segments
weaknesses: The left boundary uses (?:(?:^|\.)) which matches either start-of-string or a literal dot, but the lookahead structure is missing — the criteria specify (?:(?:^|\.)(?=...)) or a required literal dot, and the current pattern consumes the dot rather than using a lookahead, meaning the dot before the region is consumed and not part of the named group but could cause issues with re.findall() returning the outer non-capturing group content in some edge cases

The right boundary suffix pattern (?:\.|$|/)?  makes the trailing delimiter optional with ?, which is acceptable but slightly looser than necessary — a URL ending without any of these characters would still match, though this is a minor concern

The regex has a syntax issue: the closing parenthesis of re.compile() is missing a closing quote and parenthesis in the provided solution (r'...missing closing quote), suggesting the solution as presented may not compile correctly as written
task: Create a regex pattern to match and extract AWS region codes from various AWS service endpoints

strengths: Correctly implements all 7 storage classes with proper tiered pricing for STANDARD and flat rates for others, including named constants with inline comments referencing the AWS pricing URL
Properly enforces minimum billable sizes (128KB for IA/GLACIER_IR, 48KB for GLACIER/GLACIER_DEEP_ARCHIVE) and minimum duration fractions using the max() pattern as specified

Validation logic correctly raises ValueError for negative size_bytes, non-positive num_objects, and unrecognized storage_class; docstring documents INTELLIGENT_TIERING assumptions and rate staleness warning
weaknesses: STANDARD storage class does not apply actual_duration_fraction correctly — the tiered cost is multiplied by actual_duration_fraction, but the criteria formula implies a full-month cost (no duration scaling mentioned for STANDARD), and more importantly the IA/GLACIER classes correctly use max(actual_duration_fraction, min_days/DAYS_IN_MONTH) but STANDARD simply multiplies by actual_duration_fraction without any minimum, which may be intentional but is inconsistent

The num_objects validation uses isinstance(num_objects, int) which will reject float values like 1.0 that are numerically integers, and also rejects numpy integers — this could be overly strict depending on use case, though the criteria says int type

GLACIER_IR_RATE is set to $0.004/GB which matches GLACIER rate but the actual AWS Glacier Instant Retrieval rate is $0.004/GB — this appears correct, however the criteria does not explicitly state the GLACIER_IR rate so this cannot be fully verified; minor concern that the monitoring fee for INTELLIGENT_TIERING does not scale with actual_duration_fraction (monitoring is charged per month regardless, which may be correct behavior but is ambiguous)
task: Write a Python function to calculate AWS S3 storage costs based on object size and storage class
